title: Immunopathological signatures in multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children and pediatric COVID-19

annotation_policy.scope: Focused benchmark fixture for final CSV comparison, not an exhaustive gene catalogue.
annotation_policy.included: Curated genes from the paper's central MIS-C/pediatric COVID-19 immunopathological signatures: TRBV11-2 repertoire usage, HLA class I allele-combination association, and early MIS-C soluble biomarker/proteomic signatures.
annotation_policy.excluded: Markers from broad assay panels, methods-only genes, treatment-response variables, table-only secondary analytes, clinical laboratory terms, and proteins whose mention is not a clean target gene-level output for this fixture.
annotation_policy.association_language: Use signature, biomarker, repertoire expansion, or genetic susceptibility language. Do not convert these findings into causal gene-disease claims.
annotation_policy.source_grounding: Annotated from the browser-readable Nature Medicine article page, with PMCID/PMC metadata retained because the pipeline fetches PMC/EPMC XML. The PMC browser page showed an automated browser check in this session, so evidence locations cite article sections and figures rather than line-stable PMC anchors.

expected_genes.evidence_location: Abstract; Results: TRBV11-2 usage over time in MIS-C CD4+ T cells; Figure 5
expected_genes.evidence_summary: MIS-C showed transient expansion/increased frequency of TRBV11-2 T-cell clonotypes, especially early after hospitalization, with decline over time and association with inflammatory and T-cell activation signatures.
expected_genes.notes: Primary molecular/repertoire finding; report as TCR beta variable gene usage, not as a causal mutation.

expected_genes.evidence_location: Abstract; Results: TRBV11-2 usage over time in MIS-C CD4+ T cells; Extended Data Table 3
expected_genes.evidence_summary: The combination including HLA-A*02 was enriched in the restricted Italian/European ancestry MIS-C subgroup compared with pediatric COVID-19 and controls.
expected_genes.notes: Allele-level association is HLA-A*02 as part of A*02, B*35, C*04; do not treat HLA-A alone as sufficient.

expected_genes.evidence_location: Abstract; Results: TRBV11-2 usage over time in MIS-C CD4+ T cells; Extended Data Table 3
expected_genes.evidence_summary: The combination including HLA-B*35 was enriched in MIS-C subjects in the ancestry-restricted subcohort.
expected_genes.notes: Allele-level association is HLA-B*35 as part of A*02, B*35, C*04.

expected_genes.evidence_location: Abstract; Results: TRBV11-2 usage over time in MIS-C CD4+ T cells; Extended Data Table 3
expected_genes.evidence_summary: The combination including HLA-C*04 was enriched in MIS-C subjects in the ancestry-restricted subcohort.
expected_genes.notes: Allele-level association is HLA-C*04 as part of A*02, B*35, C*04.

expected_genes.evidence_location: Abstract; Results: Soluble biomarkers in the early phase of pCOVID-19 and MIS-C; Figure 2
expected_genes.evidence_summary: Early MIS-C samples had increased IFN-gamma as part of a type II IFN signaling biomarker signature, and the paper describes prominent type II IFN-dependent signatures in MIS-C.
expected_genes.notes: Measured as IFN-gamma protein/cytokine; map to IFNG for HGNC comparison but preserve biomarker wording.

expected_genes.evidence_location: Results: Soluble biomarkers in the early phase of pCOVID-19 and MIS-C; Figure 2; Proteomic analysis/Figure 3
expected_genes.evidence_summary: CXCL9 was elevated in early MIS-C as a type II IFN signaling biomarker and also appeared among inflammatory biomarkers in proteomic profiling.
expected_genes.notes: Protein-level biomarker evidence; suitable as expected gene because the symbol is clean and central to the reported signature.

expected_genes.evidence_location: Results: Soluble biomarkers in the early phase of pCOVID-19 and MIS-C; Figure 2; Proteomic analysis/Figure 3
expected_genes.evidence_summary: CXCL10 was elevated in early MIS-C as part of the type II IFN signaling biomarker signature and was reported among increased inflammatory biomarkers in proteomic profiling.
expected_genes.notes: Protein-level biomarker evidence; do not infer direct causality.

expected_genes.evidence_location: Results: Soluble biomarkers in the early phase of pCOVID-19 and MIS-C; Figure 2; Extended Data Figure 4
expected_genes.evidence_summary: Early MIS-C showed increased IL-6 among macrophage activation biomarkers, and IL-6 decreased after glucocorticoid-containing treatment in longitudinal analyses.
expected_genes.notes: Measured as cytokine protein; include as central biomarker, not causal gene.

expected_genes.evidence_location: Results: Soluble biomarkers in the early phase of pCOVID-19 and MIS-C; Figure 2
expected_genes.evidence_summary: TNF-alpha was increased in early MIS-C within the macrophage activation biomarker group and contributed to inflammatory discrimination analyses.
expected_genes.notes: Paper uses TNF-alpha protein language; HGNC symbol is TNF.

expected_genes.evidence_location: Results: Soluble biomarkers in the early phase of pCOVID-19 and MIS-C; Figure 2
expected_genes.evidence_summary: IL-10 was elevated in early MIS-C among macrophage activation-related biomarkers and was part of random forest discrimination of pediatric COVID-19 versus controls.
expected_genes.notes: Protein biomarker; retain as signature evidence.

expected_genes.evidence_location: Results: Soluble biomarkers in the early phase of pCOVID-19 and MIS-C; Figure 2; Extended Data Table 2
expected_genes.evidence_summary: IL-15 was increased in early MIS-C and distinguished pediatric COVID-19 from MIS-C in multivariate biomarker analysis.
expected_genes.notes: Protein biomarker; also connected conceptually to IL-15 signaling response pathways in TRBV11-2 CD4+ T-cell analyses.

expected_genes.evidence_location: Results: Soluble biomarkers in the early phase of pCOVID-19 and MIS-C; Figure 2; Proteomic analysis/Figure 3
expected_genes.evidence_summary: MPO was elevated as a neutrophil activation biomarker in early MIS-C and appeared in proteomic/random forest treatment-response analyses.
expected_genes.notes: Protein-level biomarker; include because symbol is clean and central to the inflammatory panel.

expected_genes.evidence_location: Results: Soluble biomarkers in the early phase of pCOVID-19 and MIS-C; Figure 2
expected_genes.evidence_summary: MMP-9 was elevated in early MIS-C as part of matrisome-related inflammation.
expected_genes.notes: Paper reports MMP-9 protein; HGNC symbol is MMP9.

expected_genes.evidence_location: Results: Soluble biomarkers in the early phase of pCOVID-19 and MIS-C; Discussion
expected_genes.evidence_summary: Early MIS-C showed increased soluble CD163 among macrophage activation biomarkers, and CITE-seq discussion describes a MIS-C monocyte signature including increased CD163 expression.
expected_genes.notes: Measured as soluble CD163 protein and monocyte expression marker; include with biomarker caveat.

expected_genes.evidence_location: Results: Soluble biomarkers in the early phase of pCOVID-19 and MIS-C; Figure 2
expected_genes.evidence_summary: CX3CL1 was elevated in early MIS-C within the matrisome-related inflammation biomarker group.
expected_genes.notes: Protein biomarker; include as focused expected output.

expected_genes.evidence_location: Results: Soluble biomarkers in the early phase of pCOVID-19 and MIS-C; Figure 2; Longitudinal evolution of blood biomarkers/Figure 3g
expected_genes.evidence_summary: LBP was elevated in early MIS-C as a septic shock-associated biomarker and appeared in random forest treatment-response analyses.
expected_genes.notes: Protein biomarker; include because LBP is a clean HGNC symbol but avoid implying genetic susceptibility.

excluded_markers.reason: Central to pediatric COVID-19 type I IFN response, but this fixture focuses on MIS-C signatures and the seed set; not included as an expected MIS-C gene output.
Important soluble/matrisome-related markers, but IL-33 was low in pCOVID-19 and sST2 is a soluble receptor marker; kept out to avoid over-expanding the focused fixture.
Protein receptor biomarkers in macrophage activation/treatment-response analyses; not part of the curated expected list.
Soluble T-cell activation marker; protein-only panel marker here rather than a central gene-level expected output.
Listed in the macrophage activation biomarker panel but not a headline signature selected for the focused fixture.
Endothelial injury/activation protein biomarkers; excluded to keep expected rows focused and avoid broad panel exhaustiveness.
Neutrophil activation protein marker in the panel; MPO was retained as the representative neutrophil marker.
Clinical protein/lab marker with multiple subunit genes; not a clean single HGNC final CSV target for this fixture.
Inflammatory clinical/protein biomarker reported in proteomics; excluded because this fixture prioritizes immune-gene signatures over general clinical inflammation markers.
Proteomic cardiac involvement marker in MIS-C, not a central immunopathological gene signature for the focused expected list.
Autoantibody targets detected mainly with IVIG confounding; not expected gene-disease findings for final CSV comparison.
CITE-seq surface protein markers higher on TRBV11-2 CD4+ T cells, but not selected as core final gene outputs.

excluded_markers.marker: HCST, DUSP2, GZMK, PRF1, GZMA, IL32, PSMB1, HAVCR2, SIRPG, CTLA4, RAC2, MSN, ITGB2, SELL, IL2RB, IL2RG
excluded_markers.reason: Secondary single-cell gene-expression markers associated with TRBV11-2 CD4+ T-cell activation/effector pathways; useful context but beyond the focused 12-20 gene fixture.

notes: The fixture intentionally includes all 16 seed genes after source verification because each maps to a clean HGNC-level symbol and a central paper-reported MIS-C signature, allele association, or biomarker class.

Most cytokine/chemokine/matrisome entries are measured as soluble proteins. Final row comparison should accept them only when the pipeline grounds the paper's signature/biomarker language and should not score causal variant-style claims as correct.
HLA-A, HLA-B, and HLA-C should be evaluated with allele context: A*02, B*35, and C*04 as a combination, not independent single-gene causality.
TRBV11-2 should be evaluated as transient TCR repertoire usage/expansion in MIS-C, associated with inflammation and T-cell activation.
